prompt: The Dwaine fit is Yhat = -68.86 + 1.4546 X1 + 9.3655 X2, with X1 young population (thousands) and X2 disposable income (thousand dollars). Which reading of the 1.4546 slope is correct?
options: Each extra thousand young residents raises sales by 1.4546, ignoring income entirely.
Holding income fixed, each extra thousand young residents is associated with about 1.4546 thousand dollars more predicted sales.
Population explains 1.4546 times as much variation as income.
A city with 1000 young residents is predicted to sell 1.4546 thousand dollars.
why: In multiple regression a slope is a partial effect: it is the change in predicted sales for a one-unit rise in that predictor with the OTHER predictors held fixed. So 1.4546 means about 1,455 dollars more sales per extra thousand young residents at a fixed income, not an unconditional effect and not a ratio of importances. See section 7.1 (@ch07-ls-matrix).

prompt: Order the steps that turn raw Dwaine data into the coefficient vector b by matrix least squares.
steps: Build the design matrix X: a column of ones, then the two predictor columns
Form the cross products X'X (3 by 3) and X'Y (3 by 1)
Invert the small p by p matrix X'X
Multiply to solve the normal equations: b = (X'X)^-1 X'Y
why: Section 7.1 (@ch07-ls-matrix) derives b = (X'X)^-1 X'Y from the normal equations X'X b = X'Y. You build X with its leading ones column, form the two cross products, invert the small 3 by 3 X'X (never the 21 by 21 XX'), and multiply. One formula produces all three coefficients at once.

prompt: These are the first six Toluca lots (lot size, hours). Drag the line to make total squared error as small as possible: this is exactly what least squares does by projection.
why: Least squares picks the fit whose residual vector is shortest, the perpendicular from Y onto the column space of X (section 7.2, @ch07-geometry). Minimizing the sum of squared vertical gaps is that same projection seen from the data. On this six-lot slice the best line even dips slightly below zero at lot size zero: least squares minimizes squared error, not interpretability.

prompt: Each dot is a Dwaine city in predictor space. Tap the highest-leverage city: the one sitting farthest into the far corner, unusual on population AND income together.
why: Leverage h_ii is the diagonal of the hat matrix H = X(X'X)^-1 X' (section 7.3, @ch07-hat-matrix); it measures a city's distance from the joint center of the predictor cloud. Row 20 (population 82.7, income 19.1) has the largest h_ii = 0.279, higher than cities with a bigger population alone, because it is extreme on both predictors at once. Reading leverage off one axis is the common mistake.

prompt: Match each hat-matrix object to what it does or equals.

pairs.meaning: equals p, the number of parameters
equals n - p, the error degrees of freedom

why: The hat matrix H projects Y onto the fitted values and I - H onto the residuals; both are symmetric and idempotent. Its trace counts parameters, tr(H) = p, so tr(I - H) = n - p (section 7.3, @ch07-hat-matrix). Those two facts drive the covariance of b, the n - p divisor, and Gauss-Markov.

prompt: One sentence about the least-squares estimator b is wrong. Tap it.

sentences.text: b is unbiased: E{b} = beta, so on average it hits the true coefficients.
Cov{b} = sigma^2 (X'X)^-1, and the standard errors are square roots of its diagonal times MSE.
b needs normal errors to be the best linear unbiased estimator.
Among all linear unbiased estimators, b has the smallest variance.

why: The Gauss-Markov theorem (section 7.6, @ch07-gauss-markov) proves b is BLUE using only mean-zero, equal-variance, uncorrelated errors. Unbiasedness, the covariance sigma^2 (X'X)^-1, and the BLUE optimality all hold with NO normality assumption. Normality is a separate, stronger claim that buys the exact normal sampling distribution and the t and F tests of Chapter 8.

prompt: The Dwaine model has n = 21, p = 3, SSE = 2180.93. A classmate reports s^2 = 2180.93 / 21 = 103.9. What is the correct estimate of sigma^2, and why?
options: 103.9: dividing by n is right because there are 21 observations.
2180.93 / 20 = 109.0: use n - 1, as for a sample variance.
2180.93 / 18 = 121.16: divide by n - p, the error degrees of freedom, so MSE is unbiased.
2180.93 / 3 = 726.98: divide by the number of parameters p.
why: Fitting p = 3 coefficients uses up 3 of the 21 directions in the data, leaving n - p = 18 for the residuals. Only dividing by n - p makes E{MSE} = sigma^2, because tr(I - H) = n - p (section 7.5, @ch07-sigma2). The classmate's 103.9 divides by n and is biased low; the correct MSE is 121.16.
